title: Lime marmalade
subtitle: Bitter-fresh marmalade for breakfast.
excerpt: A kilo of limes shredded fine and steeped overnight before cooking down with sugar to a clear, dark amber jelly. Sharper than Seville-orange, fragrant and sea-green when held to the light. Six jars from one slow afternoon.
sourceNotes: The British marmalade tradition, codified through Mrs Beeton (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) and Eliza Acton's earlier Modern Cookery (1845). The lime version is the West Indian variant, brought into the British marmalade canon in the late nineteenth century.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubling is reasonable; tripling pushes the limits of most jam pans. Use a 12-litre jam pan for double batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Steeped fruit must sit overnight or 24 hours before the second cook. Jars need to cool fully before storing.
recipe.temperatureNote: hob — rolling boil at setting point

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which the pectin and sugar in jam or marmalade reach a state that will gel as it cools. Conventionally 104°C on a sugar thermometer; the cold-saucer wrinkle test is the backup.

content.text: Limes are sharper and more aromatic than Seville oranges and produce a marmalade with a darker, deeper colour. The technique is identical to a Seville: rinds shredded fine, pith and pips tied in a muslin bag for pectin, the whole lot steeped overnight in water. Next day it cooks down for an hour, sugar goes in, and the marmalade is boiled hard to setting point: 104°C.

Setting point is the only thing to watch. A sugar thermometer is the reliable test. The backup, and the canonical British grandmother's method, is the cold-saucer wrinkle: drop a teaspoon of marmalade onto a chilled saucer, wait a minute, push with a fingertip. A skin that wrinkles means setting point. A skin that slides means more boiling.
What you need
A heavy 8 to 10 litre jam pan. Six 340 g preserving jars with lids, sterilised. A sugar thermometer that clips to the side of the pan. A square of muslin cloth and butcher's twine for the pith bag.
Method
Prepare the fruit (day one)

Halve each {{lime}} of lime and squeeze the juice into a large bowl. Tip the squeezed halves onto a board. With a small sharp knife, scoop out the pith and pips from each half and tip into a square of muslin. Slice the cleaned lime rinds into fine shreds, 2 to 3 mm thick. Tip the shreds and the juice into the jam pan.
Tie the muslin bag tightly with string and drop into the pan. Add {{water}} of water and the juice of {{lemon}} of lemon. Cover and let stand overnight at cool room temperature.
First cook (day two)

Bring the pan to a gentle simmer. Cook for 60 to 70 minutes, partly covered, until the rinds are completely soft and you can press them between thumb and finger without resistance. The liquid should have reduced by roughly a third.
Squeeze the pectin bag

Lift the muslin bag out onto a heatproof plate. Let it cool 10 minutes, then squeeze hard with clean hands or between two saucers; the pectin-rich pulp should run back into the pan in thick strings. Discard the spent bag.
Add the sugar

Tip {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar into the pan. Stir gently over low heat until every grain has dissolved; this is the moment to be patient, as undissolved sugar crystals seed graininess in the finished marmalade. Run a finger up the back of the wooden spoon; it should feel smooth, not gritty.
Boil to setting point

Turn the heat to high. Clip in a sugar thermometer. Bring to a rolling boil. Cook for 12 to 20 minutes; do not stir the boiling marmalade except to scrape any sugar from the sides. The marmalade is at setting point when the thermometer reads 104°C and the surface has the look of a thick syrup with large slow bubbles. Confirm with the cold-saucer test: drop a teaspoon onto a chilled saucer, wait 1 minute, push with a fingertip. Wrinkled skin means setting point reached. If it slides, boil another 3 minutes and test again.
Rest and jar

Take off the heat. Let stand for 10 minutes; this lets the peel distribute through the marmalade rather than floating to the top of the jars. Ladle into the warm sterilised jars, leaving 5 mm of headspace. Seal at once. Cool fully on a wire rack and store in a cool dark cupboard for up to a year. Refrigerate after opening.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Tip the runny marmalade back into the jam pan and boil another 5 to 10 minutes. Re-test with the cold-saucer method. The thermometer is the most reliable guide; 104°C is the target.

items.symptom: Peel floating at the top of the jars
items.fix: Let the cooked marmalade rest 10 minutes off the heat before ladling. As the syrup thickens, the peel distributes evenly.

items.symptom: Sugar crystals in the finished marmalade
items.cause: Sugar not fully dissolved before the rolling boil.
items.fix: Stir gently over low heat until no grit is felt on the back of the spoon. Pour boiled marmalade quickly and avoid splashing on the inside walls of the jars.

items.cause: Boiled too long after setting point reached.
items.fix: Use a thermometer to pull the pan off the heat the moment 104°C is reached. Citrus marmalade burns and darkens fast in the last 2 minutes.

content.text: Where this dish lives

Marmalade is the British breakfast preserve, spread on hot buttered toast and served alongside a pot of strong tea. The Seville-orange version is the classic; the lime version comes from the West Indies, where the fruit grows year-round, and from English households who wanted a sharper taste than orange could give. A spoonful on the table, a wedge of cheddar on the side, and a slice of brown bread underneath.
